back_translation: On the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar, the day the king's command and decree were to take effect — the day the enemies of the Jews had hoped to have power over them — it was reversed, so that the Jews had power over those who hated them.

The Jews assembled in their cities throughout all the provinces of King Ahasuerus to deal with those who sought their harm. No one could withstand them, because the fear of them had fallen upon all peoples.
All the officials of the provinces, the satraps, the governors, and the king's administrators helped the Jews, because the fear of Mordecai had fallen upon them.
For Mordecai was great in the palace, and his fame spread throughout all the provinces, for this man Mordecai grew more and more powerful.
So the Jews struck down all their enemies with the sword, killing and destroying, and did as they pleased to those who hated them.
In the citadel of Susa the Jews killed and destroyed five hundred men,
including Parshandatha, Dalphon, Aspatha,
Poratha, Adalia, Aridatha,
Parmashta, Arisai, Aridai, and Vaizatha,
the ten sons of Haman son of Hammedatha, the enemy of the Jews. They killed these, but they did not lay a hand on the plunder.
On that day the number of those killed in the citadel of Susa was reported to the king.

The king said to Queen Esther, 'In the citadel of Susa the Jews have killed and destroyed five hundred men, including Haman's ten sons. What then have they done in the rest of the king's provinces? Now what further is your petition? It will be granted. And your request? It will be done.'
Esther replied, 'If it pleases the king, let the Jews in Susa also be allowed to carry out today's decree tomorrow, and let the bodies of Haman's ten sons be hanged on the gallows.'
So the king commanded that this be done. A decree was issued in Susa, and they hanged the ten sons of Haman.
The Jews in Susa assembled again on the fourteenth day of the month of Adar and killed three hundred men in Susa, but they did not lay a hand on the plunder.

The rest of the Jews in the king's provinces also assembled to defend their lives and gained rest from their enemies, killing seventy-five thousand of those who hated them, but they did not lay a hand on the plunder.
This happened on the thirteenth day of the month of Adar, and on the fourteenth they rested, making it a day of feasting and joy.
But the Jews in Susa had assembled on both the thirteenth and the fourteenth, so they rested on the fifteenth, making it a day of feasting and joy.
This is why the rural Jews who live in the villages observe the fourteenth day of the month of Adar as a day of joy and feasting, a holiday for sending gifts to one another.
Mordecai recorded these events and sent letters to all the Jews in all the provinces of King Ahasuerus, both near and far,
to establish that they should observe the fourteenth and fifteenth days of the month of Adar every year,

as the days on which the Jews gained rest from their enemies, and the month in which their sorrow turned to joy and their mourning into a holiday — to keep them as days of feasting and joy, of sending gifts to one another and giving presents to the poor.
So the Jews adopted the custom they had begun to practice, as Mordecai had written to them.
For Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite, the enemy of all the Jews, had plotted against the Jews to destroy them, and had cast the Pur (that is, the lot) to crush and destroy them.

But when the matter came before the king, he commanded by letter that the evil scheme Haman had devised against the Jews should return upon his own head, and that he and his sons should be hanged on the gallows.
Therefore these days are called Purim, from the word Pur. Because of all the words of this letter, and because of what they had seen and experienced,
the Jews resolved to take it as a custom — for themselves, their descendants, and all who join them — that they would not fail to observe these two days at the appointed time every year.

These days were to be remembered and observed in every generation, family, province, and city, so that these days of Purim would not cease among the Jews, and their memory would not fade from their descendants.
Queen Esther daughter of Abihail, together with Mordecai the Jew, wrote with full authority to confirm this second letter concerning Purim.
And Mordecai sent letters to all the Jews in the 127 provinces of the kingdom of Ahasuerus, words of peace and truth,

to confirm these days of Purim at their appointed times, as Mordecai the Jew and Queen Esther had established them, and as they had bound themselves and their descendants concerning the matters of fasting and lamentation.
The decree of Esther confirmed these regulations of Purim, and it was written in the record.
